title: Terraform count vs for_each and Resource Reindexing

options.text: With `count`, resources are tracked by positional index (`[0]`,`[1]`,`[2]`). Removing the middle element shifts carol from index 2 to index 1, so Terraform sees `this[2]` destroyed and `this[1]` changed. Switch to `for_each` over a set/map so each resource is keyed by a stable identity (e.g. the username) rather than position.

This is expected and unavoidable; any change to a list-driven resource forces recreation of all elements after the changed index. Use `create_before_destroy` lifecycle to avoid downtime, but the recreation itself cannot be prevented.
The issue is that `count` does not support `length()`. Replace it with a hardcoded count and add a `dynamic` block so the list elements are iterated lazily without reindexing.
Run `terraform state mv` after every list edit to shift the indices manually; this is the documented and only supported way to handle ordered `count` collections.

explanation: `count` addresses instances by ordinal position in the resource address (`aws_iam_user.this[0]`, `[1]`, `[2]`). Terraform's state keys are those indices, so deleting a middle element shifts every later element down one slot; Terraform diffs by address and concludes that the shifted instances changed and the now-missing tail index must be destroyed. `for_each` over a set or map keys each instance by a stable value (`aws_iam_user.this["carol"]`), so removing `"bob"` only destroys `this["bob"]` and leaves carol untouched. (b) is wrong: the recreation is not inherent to list-driven resources, it is specific to positional indexing; `for_each` prevents it entirely. (c) is wrong: `count` works fine with `length()`, and `dynamic` blocks generate repeated nested blocks within a single resource (e.g. multiple `ingress` rules) — they are unrelated to instance identity. (d) is wrong: `state mv` can patch indices but it is an error-prone manual workaround, not the intended pattern; `for_each` is the structural fix.

hints.title: How is each instance addressed?
hints.body: Compare aws_iam_user.this[1] (count) with aws_iam_user.this["carol"] (for_each). One key is positional, the other is identity.

hints.title: What is a dynamic block for?
hints.body: dynamic blocks repeat nested configuration blocks inside one resource. They are not a way to choose how many resource instances exist.
